name: Robert F Kennedy Jr Vaccines
description: Robert F. Kennedy Jr. (RFK Jr.) is a prominent figure in the anti-vaccine movement in the United States. His views and activism center around the claim that vaccines are dangerous and linked to various health problems, particularly autism. These claims are widely refuted by the scientific and medical communities. Kennedy argues that government agencies and pharmaceutical companies are colluding to hide the risks associated with vaccines for financial gain. He founded the Children's Health Defense, an organization that promotes anti-vaccine narratives and challenges vaccine mandates. Kennedy's arguments often focus on the presence of vaccine ingredients like thimerosal (a mercury-based preservative, largely removed from childhood vaccines in the US since 2001) and aluminum, claiming these are neurotoxic and cause harm, despite scientific evidence to the contrary. He also alleges that vaccine safety testing is inadequate and that adverse events are systematically underreported. Mainstream science and medicine overwhelmingly support the safety and efficacy of vaccines, citing decades of research, rigorous testing protocols, and extensive monitoring systems. Organizations like the CDC, WHO, and AAP have consistently affirmed that vaccines are among the safest and most effective tools in preventing infectious diseases. Studies have repeatedly debunked any link between vaccines and autism, and the scientific consensus is that vaccines far outweigh any potential risks. Critics point to RFK Jr.'s selective interpretation of scientific data, reliance on anecdotal evidence, and promotion of misinformation that undermines public health efforts and creates vaccine hesitancy. His claims have been widely criticized by medical professionals, scientists, and public health officials, who argue that his rhetoric contributes to the spread of preventable diseases and erodes public trust in established medical institutions.
subtitle: Anti-vaccine activism and related claims.

claims.evidence: RFK Jr. cites retracted studies and anecdotal reports of children developing autism after vaccination.
claims.criticism: Multiple large-scale, peer-reviewed studies have found no link between vaccines and autism. The original study linking vaccines to autism was retracted due to fraudulent data, and the author was discredited. Major scientific organizations like the CDC and WHO state there is no causal relationship.

claims.claim: Thimerosal in vaccines causes neurological damage.
claims.evidence: RFK Jr. points to studies suggesting mercury is neurotoxic and argues that thimerosal, a mercury-based preservative formerly used in some vaccines, caused harm.
claims.criticism: Thimerosal has been largely removed from childhood vaccines in the US since 2001. Studies have shown no evidence that thimerosal-containing vaccines cause autism or other neurological problems. The amount of mercury in thimerosal is far below levels considered harmful.

claims.claim: Vaccine safety testing is inadequate and adverse events are underreported.
claims.evidence: RFK Jr. claims that clinical trials are biased and that the Vaccine Adverse Event Reporting System (VAERS) is not adequately monitored.
claims.criticism: Vaccine safety testing is rigorous and involves multiple phases of clinical trials. The VAERS system, while imperfect, is designed to detect potential safety signals. Adverse events are thoroughly investigated, and serious adverse events are rare. Pharmaceutical companies and regulatory agencies are required to report adverse events.

keyFigures.name: Robert F. Kennedy Jr.
keyFigures.role: Leading anti-vaccine activist and founder of Children's Health Defense.

keyFigures.name: Paul Offit
keyFigures.role: Vaccine expert and critic of RFK Jr.'s anti-vaccine claims.

timeline.event: RFK Jr. publishes an article in Rolling Stone and Salon claiming a link between thimerosal and autism (later retracted).
RFK Jr. publishes 'Thimerosal: Let the Science Speak', promoting his views on vaccine dangers.
RFK Jr. founds Children's Health Defense, an organization promoting anti-vaccine narratives.
RFK Jr. becomes a prominent voice against COVID-19 vaccines, spreading misinformation and conspiracy theories.

sources.author: Offit, Paul A.
sources.work: Vaccinated: One Man's Quest to Defeat the World's Deadliest Diseases
sources.title: Offit, Paul A.

sources.author: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
sources.title: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
